bordeaux.name: Bordeaux
bordeaux.summary: Bordeaux is France's largest fine-wine region, famed for age-worthy red blends built on Cabernet Sauvignon (Left Bank) and Merlot (Right Bank), alongside Cabernet Franc and Petit Verdot. It also produces dry whites from Sauvignon Blanc and Sémillon and world-renowned botrytized sweet wines from Sauternes and Barsac. Styles range from structured, tannic Médoc reds to plush Pomerol Merlots and honeyed dessert wines.
bordeaux.classification: Several systems coexist. The 1855 Classification ranks Médoc (plus Haut-Brion in Graves) reds into five growths (First through Fifth Growths) and Sauternes/Barsac sweet wines into Premier Cru Supérieur (Yquem alone), First and Second Growths. Graves/Pessac-Léognan has its own Crus Classés de Graves. Saint-Émilion is reclassified roughly every decade (most recently 2022) into Premier Grand Cru Classé A, Premier Grand Cru Classé B, and Grand Cru Classé. Pomerol has no official classification.

bordeaux.profile: An Atlantic-facing region on the Gironde estuary in southwest France, split by rivers into Left Bank, Right Bank and Entre-Deux-Mers. Its maritime climate is moderated by the ocean and the protective Landes pine forest, giving warm, humid summers ideal for ripening Cabernet and Merlot.

trips.summary: A classic Cabernet Sauvignon pilgrimage up the D2 'Route des Châteaux', from the elegant gravel terroirs of Margaux to the powerhouse First and Second Growths of Pauillac and Saint-Estèphe.

sudouest.name: Sud-Ouest
sudouest.summary: Southwest France is a diverse patchwork of appellations stretching from the Dordogne to the Pyrenees, prized for distinctive indigenous grapes such as Malbec (Cahors), Tannat (Madiran) and Petit/Gros Manseng (Jurançon). It produces deep, structured reds, honeyed sweet whites, crisp dry whites and traditional sparkling wines, often at excellent value. Many Bergerac-area wines echo Bordeaux blends, while the deep south offers unique styles.
sudouest.classification: Unlike Bordeaux, the Southwest has no growth hierarchy; quality is organized through its many AOC/AOP appellations: Cahors (Malbec), Madiran (Tannat) with sweet white Pacherenc du Vic-Bilh, Bergerac and Monbazillac (Bordeaux-style reds and botrytized sweet whites), Jurançon (Manseng), Gaillac (diverse), Fronton (Négrette), Marcillac (Fer Servadou), Saint-Mont, and Basque Irouléguy.

sudouest.profile: A patchwork of inland appellations (Cahors, Madiran, Gaillac, Jurançon) stretching from the Dordogne to the Pyrenean foothills. Climate shifts from oceanic-influenced near Bergerac to warmer, more continental and increasingly mountain-tempered toward the south.

visit.scenery: rolling hills, bastide towns, Pyrenees backdrop

bourgogne.name: Bourgogne
bourgogne.summary: Burgundy is the benchmark for terroir-driven Pinot Noir (reds) and Chardonnay (whites), with Aligoté and Gamay as secondary grapes. Styles range from steely, mineral Chablis in the north through the profound reds of the Côte de Nuits and the world's greatest white wines of the Côte de Beaune, to the value-driven Côte Chalonnaise and the broad, fruity whites of the Mâconnais. Wines are defined more by precise vineyard origin (climat) than by producer.
bourgogne.classification: A four-tier pyramid based on vineyard site: Régional (e.g. Bourgogne, Mâcon); Village (named after the commune, e.g. Gevrey-Chambertin, Meursault); Premier Cru (named climats within a village); and Grand Cru at the apex (33 climats, each its own appellation, e.g. Chambertin, Montrachet). Chablis follows the same hierarchy with one Grand Cru (seven climats) and many Premiers Crus.

bourgogne.profile: A narrow north-south limestone escarpment in east-central France running from Chablis through the Côte d'Or to the Mâconnais. Its semi-continental climate with cold winters and warm summers, combined with finely parcelled limestone-marl slopes, produces the world's benchmark Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.

visit.scenery: iconic walled clos, golden slopes, charming villages

beaujolais.name: Beaujolais
beaujolais.summary: Beaujolais is dominated by the Gamay grape, producing bright, juicy, low-tannin reds often made by semi-carbonic maceration on granite soils. Quality spans simple Beaujolais Nouveau and Beaujolais-Villages up to the structured, age-worthy wines of the ten northern Crus, the best of which rival Burgundy in complexity.
beaujolais.classification: Twelve AOCs in three tiers: regional Beaujolais AOC; Beaujolais-Villages (from 38 northern communes); and the ten Crus du Beaujolais, the named-village appellations that sit at the top and never carry the word 'Beaujolais' on the label.

beaujolais.profile: A hilly granite region just south of Mâcon and north of Lyon, dominated by the Gamay grape. A semi-continental climate with warm summers and granitic, schist and clay-limestone soils gives the ten crus their structure and the wider region its juicy, fruit-forward style.

visit.scenery: golden-stone villages, rolling granite hills (Pierres Dorées)

trips.name: Crus du Beaujolais Loop
trips.summary: A two-day sweep of the ten Beaujolais crus, from the granite hills of Brouilly in the south up to Fleurie and Moulin-à-Vent in the north.

jura.name: Jura
jura.summary: A small, distinctive region between Burgundy and Switzerland working five grapes: whites Savagnin and Chardonnay, and reds Poulsard (Ploussard), Trousseau and Pinot Noir. It is famous for oxidative, sherry-like wines made under a veil of yeast (sous voile), above all the intense, nutty Vin Jaune, plus sweet Vin de Paille and the fortified Macvin.
jura.classification: Four geographic AOCs (Arbois, Château-Chalon, L'Étoile, Côtes du Jura) plus three product appellations covering the whole region (Crémant du Jura, Macvin du Jura, Marc du Jura). Château-Chalon is a Vin Jaune-only appellation; Côtes du Jura is the large catch-all within which the smaller AOCs sit.

climate.growingSeason: cool; cold winters, dry warm autumns

jura.profile: A small region tucked between Burgundy and the Swiss border on the foothills of the Jura mountains. Its cold semi-continental climate and distinctive blue-grey marl and limestone soils yield singular wines, above all the oxidative Vin Jaune and Chardonnay/Savagnin/Poulsard.

savoie.name: Savoie
savoie.summary: An alpine region of fresh, light, high-acid wines, mostly white and built on the local Jacquère grape, with the aromatic Altesse (Roussette), rich Bergeron (Roussanne) and reds from Mondeuse, Gamay and Pinot Noir. Wines are typically crisp, mineral and mountain-fresh, with Chignin-Bergeron and Mondeuse offering the most depth and ageing potential.
savoie.classification: Three AOPs: Vin de Savoie (the broad appellation, which can append one of 16 named crus such as Apremont or Chignin), Roussette de Savoie (Altesse-only whites, with four crus), and Seyssel (still and sparkling). The named crus carry stricter yield and ripeness rules than the base appellation.

producers.name: Domaine des Ardoisières
producers.note: Brice Omont; Savoie's most celebrated estate, steep schist terraces

producers.name: Domaine Curtet (Marie et Florian Curtet)
producers.note: Natural-wine estate at Motz; Mondeuse and Altesse

producers.name: Maison Philippe Grisard
producers.note: Négociant-grower; broad range of Savoie crus and Mondeuse

producers.name: Cave de Cruet
producers.note: Major cooperative; broad Vin de Savoie cru range

savoie.profile: An alpine region scattered across the slopes and lake shores of the French Alps near Chambéry and Lake Geneva. A cool alpine-continental climate moderated by lakes and steep limestone scree slopes gives fresh, light wines from local grapes like Jacquère, Altesse and Mondeuse.

visit.scenery: dramatic alpine peaks, lakes, steep vineyards

champagne.name: Champagne
champagne.summary: France's premier sparkling wine region, northeast of Paris, producing wines by the traditional method (méthode champenoise) with secondary fermentation in bottle. The three principal grapes are Chardonnay (Blanc de Blancs), Pinot Noir and Pinot Meunier (together Blanc de Noirs), assembled across vintages into non-vintage cuvées or bottled as vintage and prestige cuvées.
champagne.classification: Champagne uses the Échelle des Crus ('ladder of growth'), a village-rating system from 80% to 100% established in 1919. The 17 villages rated 100% are Grands Crus and the 42 villages rated 90-99% are Premiers Crus. A crucial distinction is between Maisons/Négociants (the large Grandes Marques houses, code NM, who buy grapes) and Récoltant-Manipulant 'Grower Champagnes' (code RM, who grow and vinify their own fruit).

producers.name: Krug
producers.note: Grande Cuvée and single-vineyard Clos du Mesnil

producers.name: Louis Roederer
producers.note: Prestige cuvée Cristal

producers.name: Bollinger
producers.note: La Grande Année and R.D.

producers.note: Owner of Dom Pérignon

champagne.profile: France's northernmost major region, around Reims and Épernay, at the cool climatic limit of viticulture. Its cold semi-continental climate and chalk-dominated subsoils preserve the high acidity essential for world-class traditional-method sparkling wine.

visit.scenery: chalk cellars, grand maisons, Montagne de Reims slopes
visit.accessibility: TGV to Reims (~45min from Paris); Paris airports

trips.name: Grandes Marques: Reims & Épernay Cellars
trips.summary: A two-day tour of Champagne's most famous houses, descending into UNESCO-listed chalk crayères in Reims before tracing the grand mansions of Épernay's Avenue de Champagne. Pre-book all visits online.

stops.name: Taittinger
stops.visit: Guided tour of Gallo-Roman chalk crayères + tasting
stops.note: Cellars built into 4th-century chalk pits beneath Saint-Nicaise

stops.name: Veuve Clicquot
stops.visit: Guided crayères tour with cuvée tasting (seasonal)
stops.note: 24 km of UNESCO chalk cellars; book well ahead

stops.name: Pommery
stops.visit: Tour of monumental cellars + contemporary art
stops.note: Carved 18 m down with a dramatic sculpted staircase

stops.name: G.H. Mumm
stops.visit: Cellar tour and Cordon Rouge tasting
stops.note: Central Reims, walkable from the cathedral

stops.name: Moët & Chandon

stops.note: The flagship house on the Avenue de Champagne

stops.note: Remarkable collection of antique presses and tools

stops.name: Champagne Veuve Fourny & Fils
stops.visit: Vineyard, cellar and Premier Cru tasting, by appointment
stops.note: Active family domaine since 1856 at the southern tip of the côte

alsace.name: Alsace
alsace.summary: A long, narrow region on the eastern flank of the Vosges Mountains along the German border, producing predominantly aromatic, varietally-labelled white wines. The signature grapes are the four 'noble' varieties Riesling, Gewurztraminer, Pinot Gris and Muscat, alongside Pinot Blanc, Sylvaner and Pinot Noir (the only red); Crémant d'Alsace is the traditional-method sparkling.
alsace.classification: Alsace has three AOCs: Alsace (regional, usually varietal), Crémant d'Alsace (sparkling), and Alsace Grand Cru, which covers 51 delimited single vineyards restricted mainly to the four noble grapes. Schlossberg was the first Grand Cru (1975); Kaefferkopf the most recent (2007); Rangen is the only volcanic site. Sweet wines are labelled Vendanges Tardives (late harvest) and Sélection de Grains Nobles (botrytised).

producers.name: Trimbach
producers.note: Clos Sainte Hune and Cuvée Frédéric Émile Riesling

alsace.profile: A narrow strip on the eastern flank of the Vosges mountains along the Rhine, bordering Germany. The Vosges rain shadow makes it one of France's driest, sunniest regions, with a complex mosaic of soils ideal for aromatic Riesling, Gewurztraminer and Pinot Gris.

visit.scenery: half-timbered villages, Vosges foothills, Route des Vins

trips.name: Route des Vins: Colmar to Riquewihr
trips.summary: Two days winding through the prettiest villages of the Alsace Wine Route, mixing welcoming walk-in tasting rooms with appointment-only biodynamic domaines among half-timbered houses and Grand Cru hillsides.

stops.name: Domaine Emile Beyer
stops.visit: Tasting room in a 16th-century house on the central square
stops.note: Family estate since 1580 in one of France's loveliest villages

stops.name: Maison Léon Beyer

stops.note: Historic Eguisheim négociant-vigneron

stops.name: Maison Hugel (Hugel & Fils)
stops.visit: Walk-in tasting, no booking needed
stops.note: Established 1639; centre of the walled village

stops.name: Domaine Bott-Geyl

stops.note: Organic/biodynamic estate with six Grands Crus

stops.name: Maison Trimbach
stops.visit: Tasting room for the legendary Clos Ste-Hune Riesling
stops.note: Benchmark Alsace house, trading since 1626

loire.name: Vallée de la Loire
loire.summary: France's longest wine region, stretching ~1,000 km along the Loire river from the Atlantic to the Massif Central, and the largest producer of white and rosé wines in the country. Its emblematic grapes are Chenin Blanc (dry to sweet and sparkling), Sauvignon Blanc (Sancerre, Pouilly-Fumé), Cabernet Franc (Chinon, Bourgueil, Saumur-Champigny) and Melon de Bourgogne (Muscadet).
loire.classification: The Loire is organised by appellation rather than a vineyard cru hierarchy, with around 50 AOCs grouped in four sub-regions: Pays Nantais (Muscadet), Anjou-Saumur (Chenin Blanc and Cabernet Franc; sweet Coteaux du Layon and Quarts de Chaume Grand Cru, dry Savennières), Touraine (Vouvray and Montlouis Chenin; Chinon, Bourgueil Cabernet Franc) and Centre-Loire (Sancerre, Pouilly-Fumé, Menetou-Salon, Quincy, Reuilly). Quarts de Chaume is the Loire's sole Grand Cru.

producers.name: Didier Dagueneau
producers.note: Cult Sauvignon Blanc; Silex and Pur Sang

loire.profile: France's longest wine region, following the Loire river from the Atlantic coast at Nantes inland to Sancerre. Climate grades from cool oceanic in the west to semi-continental in the east, producing diverse styles across limestone (tuffeau), flint and schist soils.

visit.scenery: Renaissance châteaux, river valleys, troglodyte cellars

trips.name: Touraine Troglodyte Route: Vouvray, Chinon & Bourgueil
trips.summary: A Chenin-and-Cabernet-Franc loop through the tufa hills west of Tours, tasting in 10th-century troglodyte cellars carved into the limestone. Sparkling and dry Vouvray, then the reds of Chinon and Bourgueil.

rhone.name: Vallée du Rhône
rhone.summary: France's second-largest AOC wine region, split into a steep, granite-soiled North making single-variety Syrah reds and Viognier/Marsanne/Roussanne whites, and a broad, warm South built on Grenache-led GSM blends grown on galets roulés (rolled pebbles). Styles range from age-worthy, peppery Northern Syrah and rich Southern reds to dry rosés and rare sweet Muscats.
rhone.classification: Three-tier hierarchy: regional Côtes du Rhône AOC at the base, then Côtes du Rhône Villages (with named villages), then the named Crus at the top. Northern Crus: Côte-Rôtie, Condrieu, Château-Grillet, Hermitage, Crozes-Hermitage, Saint-Joseph, Cornas, Saint-Péray. Southern Crus: Châteauneuf-du-Pape, Gigondas, Vacqueyras, Cairanne, Rasteau, Beaumes-de-Venise, Vinsobres, Lirac and the rosé-only Tavel.

producers.name: E. Guigal

rhone.profile: A long river corridor from Vienne to Avignon, sharply divided into the steep continental Northern Rhône and the broad, Mediterranean Southern Rhône. Syrah dominates the granite terraces of the north while Grenache-led blends thrive on the warm, stony soils of the south.

visit.scenery: steep terraced cliffs (north), galet vineyards & Mont Ventoux (south)

trips.name: Northern Rhône Syrah & Viognier
trips.summary: Granite-hill Syrah from Hermitage plus Côte-Rôtie and Condrieu Viognier, walking the famous slopes between tastings. Big-name houses with proper tasting rooms, two estates by appointment.

provence.name: Provence
provence.summary: Mediterranean region world-famous for pale, dry, herb-scented rosé built on Grenache, Cinsault, Syrah and Mourvèdre, accounting for the bulk of production. Beyond rosé it makes serious Mourvèdre-driven reds (above all in Bandol) and distinctive whites from Clairette, Rolle (Vermentino) and Ugni Blanc.
provence.classification: Headed by the large Côtes de Provence AOC (with named sub-zones such as Sainte-Victoire, La Londe, Fréjus), plus Coteaux d'Aix-en-Provence and Coteaux Varois en Provence. The prestige reds come from small coastal crus: Bandol (Mourvèdre), Cassis (white-focused), Bellet (above Nice) and tiny Palette near Aix.

producers.note: Champion of Mourvèdre; single-vineyard La Tourtine, Cabassaou

provence.profile: A sun-drenched region between the Rhône delta and the Côte d'Azur, the world's reference for dry rosé. A classic Mediterranean climate with abundant sunshine, the cooling Mistral and limestone-schist soils suits Grenache, Cinsault and Mourvèdre, with Bandol a serious red enclave.

trips.name: Bandol Mourvèdre & Cassis Whites
trips.summary: Age-worthy Mourvèdre reds and serious rosé on Bandol's sea-facing terraces, then crisp coastal whites in the cliffside amphitheatre of Cassis. Two Bandol icons plus a Calanques estate.

stops.name: Domaine Tempier

trips.name: Côtes de Provence Rosé Trail
trips.summary: The world's most famous Provence rosés, from Gassin near Saint-Tropez to the inland Var estates. Polished tasting rooms welcoming walk-ins and booked visits.

languedoc.name: Languedoc-Roussillon
languedoc.summary: France's largest vineyard expanse, sweeping the Mediterranean arc from the Rhône delta to the Spanish border, producing predominantly Grenache-, Syrah-, Mourvèdre- and Carignan-based reds and rosés plus aromatic whites. Roussillon, Catalan in culture, is the heartland of vins doux naturels (fortified sweet wines) such as Banyuls and Maury alongside concentrated dry reds.
languedoc.classification: A patchwork of AOCs under the umbrella Languedoc AOC, with rising terroir crus and named sub-zones: Pic Saint-Loup, Terrasses du Larzac, La Clape, Faugères, Saint-Chinian, Minervois (and cru Minervois-La Livinière), Corbières (and Corbières-Boutenac), Fitou, plus Limoux for sparkling. In Roussillon: Côtes du Roussillon (Villages), the dry red cru Collioure, and fortified VDN appellations Banyuls, Maury and Rivesaltes. Much top-quality wine is sold as IGP Pays d'Oc.

producers.name: Mas de Daumas Gassac
producers.note: Languedoc's original 'grand cru'; Cabernet-based reds and whites

languedoc.profile: France's largest vineyard, sweeping along the Mediterranean arc from Nîmes to the Spanish border. A hot, dry Mediterranean climate with relentless sunshine and varied schist, limestone and galet soils underpins robust Grenache-Syrah-Mourvèdre reds and fortified Roussillon wines.

visit.scenery: garrigue hills, coast, Cathar castles, Pyrenees (Roussillon)

trips.name: Pic Saint-Loup & Terrasses du Larzac Garrigue Route
trips.summary: Inland from Montpellier into the wild garrigue beneath the Pic Saint-Loup, tasting structured Syrah-Grenache reds at historic estates framed by Mediterranean scrubland.

stops.name: Château de Lascaux
stops.visit: Estate and 12th-century priory cellar visit, tasting of five Pic Saint-Loup wines
stops.note: Organic and biodynamic, garrigue setting

stops.name: Mas de Daumas Gassac
stops.visit: Tour of the wild valley and underground cellars, tasting of the Grands Vins
stops.note: The cult 'Lafite of the Languedoc'; visits in EN/FR

trips.name: Côte Vermeille: Banyuls, Collioure & La Clape
trips.summary: The seaside fortified-wine coast where terraced vines tumble to the Mediterranean. Taste sweet Banyuls and Collioure reds in cliffside cellars, then a La Clape estate on the road back toward Narbonne.

stops.name: Terres des Templiers (Cellier des Templiers)
stops.visit: Free film, guided tour of the ageing cellars, commented tasting of the three AOPs
stops.note: Flagship Banyuls co-operative

stops.name: Cave l'Étoile
stops.visit: Tasting and cellar visit at the historic 1921 growers' co-operative
stops.note: Old-school oxidative Banyuls in outdoor solera jars

stops.name: Domaine du Mas Blanc (Dr Parcé)
stops.visit: Tasting at the sea-view cellar with single-vineyard Collioure and traditional Banyuls
stops.note: Patriarch estate behind the Collioure appellation

stops.name: Château l'Hospitalet (Gérard Bertrand)
stops.visit: Tasting boutique open daily showcasing La Clape and Languedoc cuvées
stops.note: Wine-resort estate in the La Clape massif

corse.name: Corse
corse.summary: A mountainous Mediterranean island whose wines are defined by indigenous grapes: the Sangiovese-related Nielluccio and the peppery, aromatic Sciaccarello for reds and rosés, and Vermentino (Vermentinu) for crisp, saline whites. Styles span structured age-worthy reds, fresh rosés and the rare sweet Muscat du Cap Corse.
corse.classification: Nine AOCs: Patrimonio (the island's first, 1968) and Ajaccio as the two flagship standalone crus, the sweet Muscat du Cap Corse, plus the regional Vin de Corse AOC with five geographic village denominations: Calvi, Figari, Sartène, Porto-Vecchio and Coteaux du Cap Corse. Much expressive wine is bottled as IGP Île de Beauté.

producers.name: Domaine Antoine Arena
producers.note: Iconic biodynamic Patrimonio; Carco, Grotte di Sole

producers.name: Clos Canarelli
producers.note: Leading southern estate reviving native varieties

producers.name: Domaine Gentile

producers.name: Domaine Giudicelli

producers.name: Clos d'Alzeto

producers.name: Domaine Pieretti

producers.flagship: Muscat du Cap Corse (Coteaux du Cap Corse)

corse.profile: A mountainous Mediterranean island between France and Italy, ringed by coastal and hillside vineyards. A sunny maritime Mediterranean climate tempered by sea breezes and altitude, with granite in the west/south and schist in the east, suits indigenous Nielluccio, Sciaccarello and Vermentino.

visit.scenery: mountains meeting sea, beaches, maquis, hill towns
